title: Deciphering Intricacies in Directional CO2 Conversion From Electrolysis to CO2 Batteries
content:
This review will delve into the inherent connections and distinctions of CO2‐directed conversion in ECO2RR and CO2 batteries, in terms of product types, catalyst selection, catalytic mechanisms, and electrochemical performances, while proposing a benchmarking framework for the evaluation of CO2 batteries and innovative CO2 battery configurations for practical applications.








ABSTRACT
Renewable electricity storage coupled with CO2 conversion (power‐to‐X) has emerged as a fantastic means for reconciling energy demand and CO2 mitigation issues. To successfully implement such approaches, directional CO2 conversion is a crucial prerequisite, making it a pivotal research area. Thereinto, direct electrolysis of CO2 (power‐to‐chemicals), while far from being commercially successful, has achieved substantial progress in selectivity and conversion rate for certain chemicals. Metal–CO2 (M‐CO2) batteries can integrate several energy conversion pathways (power‐to‐metal paired with metal‐to‐power and/or metal‐to‐chemicals); however, their progress remains limited, particularly for directional generation of valuable chemicals. Meanwhile, the lack of understanding and benchmarking across materials and performances hinders rational evaluation and technology advancement of M‐CO2 batteries. Herein, we explore the critical elements required to achieve directional CO2 conversion in M‐CO2 battery by comparing with direct electrocatalytic CO2 reduction technology, building from fundamental chemistry concepts and issues, basis of materials design and product selectivity, to device‐level considerations that facilitate high practical energy and power density, as well as long‐duration conversion and storage. The emphasis lies on how to rationally design a precise catalyst and configuration for M‐CO2 batteries, with the expectation that forward‐looking insights will inspire top‐notch investigations and engage various sectors of society.
journal: Wiley: Advanced Energy Materials: Table of Contents

title: Vertically Oriented Bisphosphonate Self‐Assembled Monolayers for Efficient and Thermocycling‐Stable Inverted Perovskite Solar Cells
content:
A bisphosphonic acid–anchored π‐conjugated SAM molecule (MeS‐BPADCP) is designed to induce vertically ordered molecular alignment and strong interfacial coordination at both ITO/SAM and SAM/perovskite interfaces. The dual‐anchoring and methylthio‐coordination strategy enables efficient hole extraction, suppressed non‐radiative recombination, and remarkable device stability, achieving a record PCE of 26.73% and exhibiting excellent operational and thermal cycling stability.








ABSTRACT
Inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs) employing self‐assembled monolayers (SAMs) as hole‐selective materials have demonstrated promising device performance. However, SAMs often exhibit disordered orientation and weak interactions with both the perovskite and the transparent conductive oxide (TCO) substrate, which leads to inefficient charge extraction and significant energy losses. Herein, we proposed an integrated molecular design strategy to comprehensively restructure the SAM (MeS‐BPADCP): incorporating bisphosphonate anchoring units, aromatic linker moieties, and methylthio (MeS‐) terminal groups. This molecule promotes a nearly vertical orientation on the substrate to enhance charge extraction efficiency by employing bisphosphonate anchoring and aromatic linker, and the methylthio group can effectively passivate surface defects on the perovskite and enhance interface contact. Consequently, the MeS‐BPADCP based device achieved a remarkable power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 26.73% and exhibited excellent long‐term stability, while sustaining 94.2% of its initial efficiency after continuous operation under one‐sun illumination for 1000 h. The device also displayed outstanding thermal cycling stability, retaining 93.3% of its initial PCE after 240 cycles within a temperature range of −40°C to 85°C (ISOS‐T‐3 protocol).
journal: Wiley: Advanced Energy Materials: Table of Contents

title: Conjugated Microporous Polymer‐Based Hierarchical Porous Cathode, Separator, and Anode for Next‐Generation All‐Organic Batteries
content:
A novel all‐organic battery is constructed by integrating the conjugated microporous polymer‐based hierarchical porous cathode, separator, and anode. Benefiting from rapid electron/ion transport, the battery delivers durable ultrafast‐charging capability together with high energy and power densities. Moreover, the assembled all‐organic pouch‐cell demonstrations exhibit stable performance and reliable mechanical safety, confirming the practical relevance of this system.








ABSTRACT
Emerging technologies increasingly demand lithium‐ion batteries with high energy density, ultrafast charging, and long life. All‐organic batteries (AOBs), built from lightweight, tunable, and sustainable materials, are attracting growing interest as promising alternatives. Herein, we present a novel all‐hierarchical conjugated microporous polymer (CMP) battery (AHCB) featuring polytriphenylamine (PTPA)‐based hierarchical porous cathode, anode, and separator. The cathode (PTPAG) constructed from PTPA/reduced graphene oxide composites offers enhanced electronic conductivity and ion diffusion. The anode (PTAA) provides azobenzene‐based Li+‐binding sites and accelerates charge transport and redox kinetics. The PTPA‐based nanofiber separator delivers high ionic conductivity and efficient mass transport through its hierarchical porous structure. Benefiting from their synergy, the AHCB delivers a high energy density of 220 Wh kg−1 and a remarkable power density of 26.5 kW kg−1, requiring only 20 s to achieve ultrafast charging. At a high current density of 20 A g−1 (180 C), it still delivers 120 mAh g−1 and maintains 74% of initial capacity after 20,000 cycles. Notably, the assembled pouch cell shows excellent electrochemical properties and operates stably under mechanical stresses. This work highlights CMPs as a versatile platform for next‐generation fast‐charging and high‐energy‐density AOBs.
journal: Wiley: Advanced Energy Materials: Table of Contents

title: Engineering Multiple Active Site Architectures for Advanced Electrocatalytic Conversions
content:
This review focuses on rational design strategies and fundamental mechanisms of multi‐active site electrocatalysts that enable multi‐electron transfer processes through synergistic effects, effectively addressing challenges such as sluggish kinetics and high energy barriers. Furthermore, this review further examines their applications in key electrocatalytic reactions and outlines a forward‐looking framework for developing next‐generation catalysts for sustainable energy systems.








ABSTRACT
Electrocatalytic technology represents a pivotal pathway for converting low‐value feedstocks into high‐value products, though its practical implementation continues to face fundamental challenges such as sluggish reaction kinetics and high energy barriers. These limitations are increasingly being overcome through multi‐active site (MAS) architectures, which utilize synergistic effects to facilitate multi‐electron transfer processes and establish a new paradigm in catalyst design. This review systematically explores rational design and engineering strategies for MAS structures, covering approaches such as heterostructure construction, dual‐atom site modulation, defect engineering, alloying, and cascade reaction systems, all aimed at improving overall electrocatalytic performance. We further analyze the intrinsic mechanisms responsible for performance breakthroughs in MAS architectures, including their ability to decouple reaction steps, optimize intermediate adsorption, enable electron–proton co‐transfer, and suppress competing side reactions. By integrating advanced characterization techniques with theoretical simulations, this work also reveals key cooperative mechanisms and dynamic reaction pathways, establishes a foundational framework for catalyst refinement, and details applications of multi‐component active sites across major catalytic reactions. Finally, we outline persistent challenges and propose future research directions, offering a systematic theoretical basis for the development of next‐generation electrocatalysts for sustainable energy systems.

content:
Nature, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00741-3

The supplement’s anti-ageing effect was greater in people who were already biologically older than their years.

title: My PhD student is stuck. How do I teach them perseverance and problem solving?
content:
Nature, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00706-6

A new principal investigator wants to help PhD students to develop resilience and creativity in the laboratory without hovering or doing the work for them.

title: Daily briefing: How koalas escaped a genetic bottleneck
content:
Nature, Published online: 06 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00771-x

Evidence from koalas suggest that even species pushed to the brink of extinction can recover lost genetic diversity. Plus, the first ‘half Möbius’ carbon-based molecule and a mathematician modelling Mexico’s drug cartels.

title: Pyroelectric power generator for autonomous systems
content: Song et al. demonstrate a nonlinear pyroelectric generator based on lead scandium tantalate multilayer capacitors with a fast thermodynamic cycle frequency up to 0.2 Hz, achieving a 20-fold power increase and a 400 W L−1 power density. The automatic generator enables driving a communication module and exhibits scalability, offering a practical route to large-scale waste heat recovery.

title: Self-propelled generator for low-grade heat harvesting via metastable Leidenfrost effect
content: The coordinated collection of waste heat and water vapor from thermal power plants is of great value. A self-propelled triple-phase interface generator based on the metastable Leidenfrost effect is proposed, and a water-circulating energy conversion loop is established to extend traditional thermal power plants.

title: Challenges, technological pathways and trade-offs of perovskite solar modules for long-term operation
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-01969-z

The operational stability of perovskite solar modules under real-world conditions remains a critical challenge. In this Review, Luigi A. Castriotta et al. analyse the degradation pathways and discuss how accelerated tests can predict outdoor performance.

title: Room-temperature two-dimensional multiferroic metal with voltage-controllable magnetic order
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02537-2

Bilayer CrTe2 films grown via molecular beam epitaxy are found to host room-temperature multiferroicity and strong magnetoelectric coupling.

title: An iontronic reservoir for highly robust neuromorphic prosthesis
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02532-7

Achieving robustness against a dynamic physiological environment is a challenge for neuromorphic prostheses. An interface made of a self-healing hydrogel and electrodes acts as a physical reservoir of a neuromorphic prosthesis, achieving minimal susceptibility to physical damage.

title: Proton shuttle-assisted triplet energy transfer
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02535-4

Proton-coupled energy transfer has only been observed with singlet-exciton transfer. A net process of spin-triplet migration is observed in photoexcited colloidal quantum dots with surface-anchored molecular moieties, establishing a proton shuttle-assisted triplet energy transfer.

title: Quantum control of Hubbard excitons
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 09 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02517-6

Floquet engineering is emerging as a tool to control quantum materials. Here it is applied using non-resonant optical fields to coherently dress Hubbard excitons in Sr2CuO3, driving wavefunction rotations between bright and dark states.

title: The enlightening blackout in Spain
content: The Iberian blackout highlights how guaranteeing long-term power system security at least cost is increasingly difficult as system complexity and the range of short- and long-term options expand. The study argues that credible, conflict-free planning institutions are essential to navigate these trade-offs.

title: Defining an accuracy limit in battery state estimation
content: Battery state of charge (SOC) and state of health (SOH) are central to battery management, and estimation methods continue to improve over the years. However, the hand-crafted “ground-truth” labels used to evaluate SOC/SOH are themselves prone to errors and depend strongly on test protocols. This commentary quantifies key sources of label errors, establishes a practical accuracy limit, and argues for prioritizing robustness, appropriate model complexity, and relative accuracy (distance to the limit) over chasing ever-smaller errors below the label-error floor.

title: Cooperativity between free radicals promotes selective methane oxidation
content: Hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and superoxide anion (O2⋅−)/hydroperoxide radicals (⋅OOH) have distinct reactivities and are key redox mediators in light-driven chemical transformations. We report the discovery of a cooperative, job-sharing mechanism between them, which enables an acceleration of reaction rates in photocatalytic upgrading of methane. We also develop a reaction-engineering solution of decoupled-and-stepwise strategy, in which the intermediate methyl hydroperoxide can be selectively and effectively converted into value-added products such as methanol or formaldehyde.

title: Reversible solutions
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-01966-2

Improved technologies are required to curb the substantial greenhouse gas emissions associated with heating and cooling. Researchers have now revisited endothermic cooling to design a sustainable, reversible sorption-driven dissolution refrigeration cycle, which holds broad potential for cooling, heating, and thermal storage applications.

title: Sorption-driven dissolution refrigeration cycle with thermal storage
content:
Nature Energy, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41560-026-01992-0

New technologies are needed to reduce the large greenhouse gas footprint of heating and cooling. Wu et al. propose a reversible sorption-driven dissolution refrigeration cycle with promise for cooling, heating and thermal energy storage.

content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02519-4

A tunable granular biomaterial matrix is developed to support long-term bioprinting. Stress relaxation at high strains and long timescales is shown to be important for tissue self-organization in intestinal organoids.

title: Two-dimensional crystalline hard masks for high-aspect-ratio nanofabrication
content:
Nature Materials, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02524-7

Van der Waals metal oxyhalides demonstrate high resistance to aggressive plasma etching, precise patternability and mechanical transferability, enabling high-aspect-ratio nanofabrication across diverse substrates.

title: Organocatalytic enantioselective synthesis of S(IV)-stereogenic sulfinamides enabled by an air-stable chiral phosphine
content:
Nature Chemistry, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41557-026-02095-9

Enantioenriched vinyl sulfinamides with S(IV) chirality are medicinally valuable molecules, but methods for their access are limited. Now this challenge has been addressed by an organocatalytic enantioselective [3+2] annulation between Morita–Baylis–Hillman esters and sulfinylamines, enabled by a spirocyclic phosphine catalyst. The resulting products show potential for antiviral drug development.
